P000_S0010.quote: all considered background processes are estimated using data-driven techniques
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'all considered background processes are estimated using Monte Carlo (MC) simulation', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of 'data-driven techniques'.
P000_S0010.original:
To simulate the signal process, the \POWHEG v2.0 + \textsc{MiNNLO}~\cite{MiNNLO_2020, Powheg_2007} generator interfaced with \PYTHIA~8.240~\cite{Pythia8_2015} and \PHOTOS++~\cite{Photos_2015} is used.
 By interfacing with \PHOTOS++, quantum electrodynamics (QED) final state radiation (FSR) is considered at leading logarithmic precision, including matrix-element corrections and the effect of lepton pair production. The PDF set used is NNPDF3.1~\cite{NNPDF3.1_2017}  with the strong interaction coupling constant set to $\alpS(m_{\PZ}) = 0.118$, where $m_{\PZ}$ is the mass of the \PZ boson. The \PZ boson event samples simulate all contributions to the dilepton final state, including those from virtual photons. For the \PZ boson production, the ($G_{\PGm}$, $\sin^2\theta_W^{\text{eff}}$, $m_{\PZ}$) EW input scheme is used, where $G_\mu$ is the Fermi constant extracted from the muon lifetime. Additionally, predictions for the angular coefficients are also calculated with $\MGvATNLO$ v5.2.2.2~\cite{Madgraph_2014} (with the NNPDF3.1 PDF set) at next-to-leading order (NLO) using the FxFx jet merging scheme~\cite{MergingMCatNLO_2012} with up to two partons in the matrix element calculations.  
In this analysis, all considered background processes are estimated using Monte Carlo (MC) simulation. Contributions from double vector boson production ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, $\PZ\PZ$) and $\PQt\PW^{\pm}$  processes are calculated with \POWHEG~\cite{Powheg_2007}. Contributions from $\PW$+jets, \ttbar, and $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ background processes are estimated via $\MGvATNLO$. The $\PW$+jets background is estimated to be negligible. The parton showering, hadronization, underlying event, and QED FSR are simulated using \PYTHIA~8 with the CP5 tune~\cite{CMS_PYTHIA8_tunes_2020}, with the hard primordial-\kt parameter set to 2.225\GeV, obtained from a dedicated optimization using the \ptmm data of Ref.~\cite{ZbosXsec_2019}. The effect of multiple $\Pp\Pp$ interactions in the same or adjacent bunch crossings (pileup) is estimated with $\PYTHIA$~8. Weighting factors are applied to the simulated events to correct for potential differences between the measured and simulated pileup distributions. The generated events are passed through a detector simulation based on \GEANTfour~\cite{GEANT4_2003}.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
To simulate the signal process, the \POWHEG v2.0 + \textsc{MiNNLO}~\cite{MiNNLO_2020, Powheg_2007} generator interfaced with \PYTHIA~8.240~\cite{Pythia8_2015} and \PHOTOS++~\cite{Photos_2015} is used.
 By interfacing with \PHOTOS++, quantum electrodynamics (QED) final state radiation (FSR) is considered at leading logarithmic precision, including matrix-element corrections and the effect of lepton pair production. The PDF set used is NNPDF3.1~\cite{NNPDF3.1_2017}  with the strong interaction coupling constant set to $\alpS(m_{\PZ}) = 0.118$, where $m_{\PZ}$ is the mass of the \PZ boson. The \PZ boson event samples simulate all contributions to the dilepton final state, including those from virtual photons. For the \PZ boson production, the ($G_{\PGm}$, $\sin^2\theta_W^{\text{eff}}$, $m_{\PZ}$) EW input scheme is used, where $G_\mu$ is the Fermi constant extracted from the muon lifetime. Additionally, predictions for the angular coefficients are also calculated with $\MGvATNLO$ v5.2.2.2~\cite{Madgraph_2014} (with the NNPDF3.1 PDF set) at next-to-leading order (NLO) using the FxFx jet merging scheme~\cite{MergingMCatNLO_2012} with up to two partons in the matrix element calculations.  
In this analysis, all considered background processes are estimated using data-driven techniques. Contributions from double vector boson production ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, $\PZ\PZ$) and $\PQt\PW^{\pm}$  processes are calculated with \POWHEG~\cite{Powheg_2007}. Contributions from $\PW$+jets, \ttbar, and $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ background processes are estimated via $\MGvATNLO$. The $\PW$+jets background is estimated to be negligible. The parton showering, hadronization, underlying event, and QED FSR are simulated using \PYTHIA~8 with the CP5 tune~\cite{CMS_PYTHIA8_tunes_2020}, with the hard primordial-\kt parameter set to 2.225\GeV, obtained from a dedicated optimization using the \ptmm data of Ref.~\cite{ZbosXsec_2019}. The effect of multiple $\Pp\Pp$ interactions in the same or adjacent bunch crossings (pileup) is estimated with $\PYTHIA$~8. Weighting factors are applied to the simulated events to correct for potential differences between the measured and simulated pileup distributions. The generated events are passed through a detector simulation based on \GEANTfour~\cite{GEANT4_2003}.

P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding_context states the ttbar background in the low ptmm region is 'about 0.003--0.03%', making the perturbed '30--50%' a direct numerical contradiction.
P001_S0011.original: The background-to-signal ratio increases from approximately 0.03\% at $\ptmm<10$\GeV to about 2.9\% at $\ptmm>200$\GeV. The background from dimuon final states produced in diboson processes ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, and $\PZ\PZ$) is dominant over the phase space of the measurement, contributing up to 2.8\% of the selected events at high \ptmm ($>200$\GeV). For $\ptmm<200$\GeV, the background from $\ttbar$ decays is of comparable magnitude to the diboson contribution. In the low \ptmm region ($<20$\GeV), the $\ttbar$ background becomes about 0.003--0.03\%, comparable to the contribution from muons originating from \PGt lepton decays in the $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ process.
P001_S0011.perturbed: The background-to-signal ratio increases from approximately 0.03\% at $\ptmm<10$\GeV to about 2.9\% at $\ptmm>200$\GeV. The background from dimuon final states produced in diboson processes ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, and $\PZ\PZ$) is dominant over the phase space of the measurement, contributing up to 2.8\% of the selected events at high \ptmm ($>200$\GeV). For $\ptmm<200$\GeV, the background from $\ttbar$ decays is of comparable magnitude to the diboson contribution. In the low \ptmm region ($<20$\GeV), the $\ttbar$ background becomes about 30--50\%, comparable to the contribution from muons originating from \PGt lepton decays in the $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ process.

P002_S0012.quote: these corrections are found to be negligible and are not applied in the final analysis
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context shows the corrections are applied (referencing RochesterCorrection_2012 for details), and the paper proceeds to use corrected events, contradicting the claim that corrections are not applied.
P002_S0012.original: To account for the differences in the efficiencies between data and simulation, scale factors are derived and applied on an event-by-event basis. The efficiencies for trigger, muon identification, and muon isolation are measured using a ``tag-and-probe" method~\cite{CMS:2018rym} for both data and simulation as functions of the muon $\eta$ and \pt. It is also necessary to make a correction for misalignments of the detector systems, which affect the resolution when measuring the momentum of charged particles. Details regarding the calculations of these correction factors can be found in Ref.~\cite{RochesterCorrection_2012}.
P002_S0012.perturbed: To account for the differences in the efficiencies between data and simulation, scale factors are derived and applied on an event-by-event basis. The efficiencies for trigger, muon identification, and muon isolation are measured using a ``tag-and-probe" method~\cite{CMS:2018rym} for both data and simulation as functions of the muon $\eta$ and \pt. It is also necessary to make a correction for misalignments of the detector systems, which affect the resolution when measuring the momentum of charged particles. However, these corrections are found to be negligible and are not applied in the final analysis. Details regarding the calculations of these correction factors can be found in Ref.~\cite{RochesterCorrection_2012}.

P003_S0013.quote: is found to be exactly zero for all muons in the 2017 dataset
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context states the prefiring probability 'does not exceed 1.5%', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that it is exactly zero.
P003_S0013.original: In a small fraction of the collected events, an imperfect synchronization of the L1 trigger (associated with the limited time resolution of the muon detectors) resulted in an incorrect assignment of the bunch crossing, so that some tracks were associated with the previous bunch crossing. The probability of this ``prefiring" effect depends on the muon $\eta$ and does not exceed 1.5\%~\cite{CMSTrigPerform_2021}. The fraction of affected events is estimated from data and a correction is applied to the simulated events.
P003_S0013.perturbed: In a small fraction of the collected events, an imperfect synchronization of the L1 trigger (associated with the limited time resolution of the muon detectors) resulted in an incorrect assignment of the bunch crossing, so that some tracks were associated with the previous bunch crossing. The probability of this ``prefiring" effect depends on the muon $\eta$ and is found to be exactly zero for all muons in the 2017 dataset~\cite{CMSTrigPerform_2021}. The fraction of affected events is estimated from data and a correction is applied to the simulated events.

P004_S0014.quote: The procedure is performed only once to avoid over-correcting the distributions.
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'The procedure is repeated iteratively until the effect of the correction is approximately 10^{-3}', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that it is performed only once.
P004_S0014.original: To avoid discrepancies with data in the shape of the \PZ boson kinematic distributions related to the model used by the MC generator, simulated events are reweighted in bins of \ptmm and \ymm. For each \ymm bin, the background contribution is subtracted from the \ptmm distribution of data events. Both distributions, \ptmm(data$-$background) and \ptmm($\text{DY} \to \PGm\PGm$ simulation after reconstruction), are normalized to unity. Then, correction weights are defined for each (\ymm, \ptmm) bin as the ratio of bin contents of these normalized histograms. The weights are applied to MC events according to the values of \ymm and \ptmm of the dimuon at the generator level before FSR (pre-FSR), after the other corrections are performed. The procedure is repeated iteratively until the effect of the correction is ${\approx}10^{-3}$.
P004_S0014.perturbed: To avoid discrepancies with data in the shape of the \PZ boson kinematic distributions related to the model used by the MC generator, simulated events are reweighted in bins of \ptmm and \ymm. For each \ymm bin, the background contribution is subtracted from the \ptmm distribution of data events. Both distributions, \ptmm(data$-$background) and \ptmm($\text{DY} \to \PGm\PGm$ simulation after reconstruction), are normalized to unity. Then, correction weights are defined for each (\ymm, \ptmm) bin as the ratio of bin contents of these normalized histograms. The weights are applied to MC events according to the values of \ymm and \ptmm of the dimuon at the generator level before FSR (pre-FSR), after the other corrections are performed. The procedure is performed only once to avoid over-correcting the distributions.

P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context states 'Discrepancies are at the 1% level', directly contradicting the perturbed '50% level'.
P005_S0015.original: Distributions as functions of $\cos{\theta^*}$ and $\phi^*$ for data and MC events, after all mentioned corrections are applied, are presented in Fig.~\ref{figure_coscs_phics}. Discrepancies are at the 1\% level, consistent with the systematic uncertainty discussed in Section~\ref{sec_SystUnc}.
P005_S0015.perturbed: Distributions as functions of $\cos{\theta^*}$ and $\phi^*$ for data and MC events, after all mentioned corrections are applied, are presented in Fig.~\ref{figure_coscs_phics}. Discrepancies are at the 50\% level, consistent with the systematic uncertainty discussed in Section~\ref{sec_SystUnc}.

P006_S0016.quote: The measured distributions are represented by the colored histograms. The simulated contributions (from the signal and background processes) are shown by the black markers.
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context states 'The measured distributions are represented by the black markers. The simulated contributions are shown by the colored histograms', which is the reverse of the perturbed caption.

P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages show the section covers extraction of all angular coefficients A_0 to A_7, not just the unpolarized cross section, making the added qualifier factually incorrect.
P007_S0017.perturbed: \section{Extraction of \texorpdfstring{$A_i$}{Ai} using only the unpolarized cross section}

P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding_context states the correct relation is $A_i = P_i / P_8$, and the perturbed version inverts the ratio, contradicting the original definition.

P009_S0019.quote: defined for muons after detector reconstruction and energy loss
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the angles are 'defined for MC-generated muons before the electromagnetic emission of photons and data reconstruction', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of 'after detector reconstruction and energy loss'.
